I'm not, Bailey, correct me if I'm wrong.
I have not read anything about sort of tables of content as being like a direct influencing factor for SEO.
What I do know is that the engagement rate, so how long the user stays on your web page, that is important for SEO.
So because Google measures the bounce rate.
So if you have that table of contents, it's easier just for people to find what they're looking for.
And stay on your website, like for longer than 10 seconds, which is what Google counts as an engaged session.
So that would be my, so it is kind of like a user experience and an SEO thing in one.
And I also think, like, if we're chunking the content more, it's useful if people are going to be reading, people are going to be scanning more because of how it appears in AI search.

So social shares don't have a direct impact on website authority.
They used to like 15 or maybe 20 years ago when SEO was still very new and then people were abusing it in forums.
So social shares are helpful as like an additional distribution channel for content, but it's not going to give us authority.
What's going to give your website authority are from like a content strategy perspective, it's the comprehensiveness of the content.
So making sure the content is helpful, original, unique, and like serves a search intent.
And then from a technical, like a SEO perspective, it's getting backlinks.
So to get backlinks, you know, you know.
We like people that are, you know, just buy links, but we can create content that's more likely to attract organic backlinks from other websites.

Usually this type of content is like, for example, compilations of different like industry statistics, those types of blog posts get linked to a lot because whenever somebody's writing a blog post, they're like always looking up statistics and they link to these types of roundup posts.

And what makes these blog posts, like they do get links and we've done this for other clients as well, but they are a little bit just more time consuming to produce for a reason that's pretty like human.
We just want to make sure that all of the stats are verified.

And then we link to those reports at the end of the blog post so that, you know, we actually reference our sources.
Because I, that, that approach can get us links.

And it's also ensuring that we're not like regurgitating stats that other people are mentioning on their site, which a lot of times those stats are like not made up, but they're super old because nobody's verifying them.
So that's like something that we could include in the content strategy.
I don't know if we talked about this last time, but, you know, when, you like officially go live with your product, the more websites.
